content:
I think this might not be possible, but maybe someone can advise me on an alternate implementation:

    pub(crate) fn set_partitionRpcPadding(&mut self, fld: [u32; 4]) {
        let byte_data: KVec<u8> = fld.iter().flat_map(|&x| x.to_le_bytes()).collect();
        self.store[208..224].copy_from_slice(&byte_data);
    }

This doesn't build because lack of FromIterator I assume, (it's just what I was going in userspace with Vec.) I pretty much just want a safe way to take a an array of sized values and copy the underlying bytes into a slice
Danilo listed a few things in his patch about the difficulties for implementing the FromIterator trait for KVec.

Yeah, the patch @Boqun Feng linked in is a good explanation.

The biggest difficulty is that FromIterator (which is required for a generic collect to properly work) doesn't handle GFP flags and allocation failures.

The GFP stuff may be solvable with PF_MEMALLOC_* flags in the long term, but infallibility is probably too heavy to lift.

I think for the long term we have to come up with a kernel specific FromIterator trait for collections to implement.
any good ideas on what might be a suitable alternate? I can just hack it for now to iterate over each u32, as I autogen this code to avoid collect or maybe just drop into unsafe

@Danilo Krummrich what's the status of this btw, and anything I could help out with? Just ran into this with some of the KMS bindings, I can workaround not having collect() but it makes my code sad lol
I worked around it in my generator code in the end

Lyude said:

Danilo Krummrich what's the status of this btw, and anything I could help out with? Just ran into this with some of the KMS bindings, I can workaround not having collect() but it makes my code sad lol

I think we need to implement our own FromIterator trait for this to work. I have this on my list, but I'll probably not get to it before somewhen next year. So, depending on how sad it make your code, feel free to pick this up. :) I'm also happy to help out with some parts too.
